pod-crashloop:oom-kill.explanation: Pod was killed due to memory limits exceeded

pod-crashloop:image-pull.explanation: Container image could not be pulled

pod-crashloop:app-crash.explanation: Application inside container is crashing - checking pod logs for the actual error
next_steps: Check pod logs: oc logs {pod} -n {ns} --previous --tail=100
Check pod events: oc describe pod {pod} -n {ns} | grep -A20 Events
Check resource limits: oc describe pod {pod} -n {ns} | grep -A5 Resources

investigation_playbook.goal: Get the crash output from previous container
Check exit code and last state
Compare resource limits with actual usage
Check pod event timeline
Is the hosting node healthy
commands: oc describe node $(oc get pod {pod} -n {ns} -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}' 2>/dev/null) 2>&1 | grep -A5 Conditions: | head -10

pod-unknown:evicted-disk-pressure.cause: Pod Evicted Due to Node Disk Pressure
pod-unknown:evicted-disk-pressure.explanation: Kubelet evicted this pod because the node ran out of disk space. Identify which node has DiskPressure and free disk or increase capacity.
next_steps: Identify the node: oc get pod <pod> -n <ns> -o wide
Check node disk: oc describe node <node> | grep DiskPressure
Check what uses disk: oc debug node/<node> -- chroot /host df -h
Review Red Hat KCS: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/4976801

investigation_playbook.goal: Which node evicted the pod
Check disk usage on the node
Which directory is consuming space
Which workload fills the disk
How many pods were evicted on this node
commands: oc get pods --field-selector spec.nodeName={node} -A --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -ci evict

pod-unknown:evicted-other.explanation: Pod was evicted by kubelet due to resource pressure on the node. Check node conditions for the specific pressure type.
next_steps: Check node conditions: oc describe node <node> | grep -A20 Conditions
Check node resource usage: oc adm top node <node>
Check events: oc get events -n <ns> --field-selector involvedObject.name=<pod>

investigation_playbook.goal: Which node and what pressure type
commands: oc describe node $(oc get pod {pod} -n {ns} -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}' 2>/dev/null) 2>&1 | grep -A20 Conditions:

investigation_playbook.goal: Node resource usage
commands: oc adm top node $(oc get pod {pod} -n {ns} -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}' 2>/dev/null) 2>&1

investigation_playbook.goal: Pod and node events

pod-crashloop:node-issue.explanation: Node became unavailable or was cordoned

pod-crashloop:kubelet-lost.explanation: Kubelet lost connection to API server

pod-crashloop:insufficient-resources.explanation: Cluster lacks resources to schedule pod

virt-handler:memory-leak.explanation: virt-handler experiencing memory leak under load

virt-handler:very-high-vm-density.explanation: Running >1000 VMs causes proportional memory growth in virt-handler. This is the primary driver of high memory usage. Known issue CNV-71448.
next_steps: Check VM distribution across nodes: oc get vmi -A -o wide --no-headers | awk '{print $4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
Consider reducing VM density per node or adding more worker nodes
Increase virt-handler memory limits to prevent OOM
Known bug: https://issues.redhat.com/browse/CNV-71448

investigation_playbook.goal: How many VMs per node
commands: oc get vmi -A -o wide --no-headers 2>&1 | awk '{print $4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: virt-handler actual memory usage per node
commands: oc adm top pod -n openshift-cnv -l kubevirt.io=virt-handler --no-headers 2>&1 | sort -k3 -rh | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Compare memory limits with actual RSS
commands: oc get ds virt-handler -n openshift-cnv -o yaml 2>&1 | grep -A5 resources:
oc adm top pod -n openshift-cnv -l kubevirt.io=virt-handler --no-headers 2>&1 | head -5

investigation_playbook.goal: Check for OOM kills
commands: oc get events -n openshift-cnv --field-selector reason=OOMKilled --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' 2>&1 | tail -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Calculate memory per VM ratio

virt-handler:moderate-vm-load.explanation: Running 500-1000 VMs - memory growth proportional to VM count. Consider spreading load across more nodes.
next_steps: Check VM distribution per node: oc get vmi -A -o wide --no-headers | awk '{print $4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
Monitor for approaching memory limits
Known bug: https://issues.redhat.com/browse/CNV-71448

investigation_playbook.goal: How many VMs per node
commands: oc get vmi -A -o wide --no-headers 2>&1 | awk '{print $4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Compare limits with actual memory
commands: oc get ds virt-handler -n openshift-cnv -o yaml 2>&1 | grep -A5 resources:
oc adm top pod -n openshift-cnv -l kubevirt.io=virt-handler --no-headers 2>&1 | sort -k3 -rh | head -5

virt-handler:high-rss-no-oom.cause: virt-handler High RSS Memory (No OOM Yet)
virt-handler:high-rss-no-oom.explanation: virt-handler pods are consuming 1-3GB RSS memory each. This is above normal baseline (~400Mi) and indicates memory growth proportional to VM count. Known issue CNV-71448.
next_steps: Check per-node VM count: oc get vmi -A -o wide --no-headers | awk '{print $4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
Check virt-handler memory limits: oc get ds virt-handler -n openshift-cnv -o yaml | grep -A5 resources
Consider increasing virt-handler memory limit if VMs per node > 50
Monitor for OOM: oc get events -n openshift-cnv --field-selector reason=OOMKilled | tail -10
Known bug: https://issues.redhat.com/browse/CNV-71448

investigation_playbook.goal: VMs per node to calculate memory/VM ratio
commands: oc get vmi -A -o wide --no-headers 2>&1 | awk '{print $4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Compare configured limits with actual RSS
commands: oc get ds virt-handler -n openshift-cnv -o yaml 2>&1 | grep -A5 resources:
oc adm top pod -n openshift-cnv -l kubevirt.io=virt-handler --no-headers 2>&1 | sort -k3 -rh | head -5

investigation_playbook.goal: How close to OOM are we
commands: oc get events -n openshift-cnv --field-selector reason=OOMKilled --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' 2>&1 | tail -10

volumesnapshot:csi-error.explanation: CSI driver failed to create snapshot

operator:mcp-degraded.explanation: MachineConfigPool nodes failed to apply updated MachineConfig - drilling down to find which node and why
next_steps: Check which nodes are degraded: oc get mcp
Check MCD logs on degraded node: oc logs -n openshift-machine-config-operator machine-config-daemon-<node> --tail=50
Check if a node is stuck in drain: oc get nodes | grep SchedulingDisabled
Review Red Hat KCS: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/5414371

investigation_playbook.goal: Which MCP is degraded and which nodes
commands: oc get mcp worker -o yaml 2>&1 | grep -A15 'degradedMachineCount\|message'

investigation_playbook.goal: Identify the specific degraded nodes
commands: oc get co machine-config -o yaml 2>&1 | grep -A10 'message:' | head -20

investigation_playbook.goal: Check MCD logs on degraded node for the error
commands: oc get pods -n openshift-machine-config-operator --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -v Running | head -10
oc logs -n openshift-machine-config-operator $(oc get pods -n openshift-machine-config-operator --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -v Running | head -1 | awk '{print $1}') --tail=50 2>&1 | tail -30

investigation_playbook.goal: MCO events timeline

operator:mcp-timeout.explanation: MachineConfig application timed out during node drain or reboot - nodes may be stuck in SchedulingDisabled

operator:mco-sync.explanation: MCO failed to sync required MachineConfigPools - check for incompatible custom MachineConfigs

operator:crashloop.explanation: Pods backing the operator are crash-looping - drilling down into pod logs
next_steps: Check pod logs for the crashing container: oc logs -n openshift-{name} <pod> --previous
Check resource limits: oc describe pod -n openshift-{name} <pod> | grep -A5 Resources
Check if node has issues: oc get node <node-of-pod>

investigation_playbook.goal: Which pod is crashing
commands: oc get pods -n openshift-{name} --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -v Running | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Get the crash output
commands: oc logs -n openshift-{name} $(oc get pods -n openshift-{name} --no-headers 2>/dev/null | grep -v Running | head -1 | awk '{print $1}') --previous --tail=50 2>&1 | tail -30

investigation_playbook.goal: Check exit code, OOM, resource limits
commands: oc describe pod -n openshift-{name} $(oc get pods -n openshift-{name} --no-headers 2>/dev/null | grep -v Running | head -1 | awk '{print $1}') 2>&1 | grep -A10 'Last State:'
oc describe pod -n openshift-{name} $(oc get pods -n openshift-{name} --no-headers 2>/dev/null | grep -v Running | head -1 | awk '{print $1}') 2>&1 | grep -A5 'Resources:'

investigation_playbook.goal: Compare resource limits with actual usage
commands: oc adm top pod -n openshift-{name} 2>&1 | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Events timeline in operator namespace

operator:resync-failed.explanation: Operator failed to resync to target version - may need manual intervention

operator:stuck-degraded.explanation: Operator is degraded and not progressing - manual investigation required

operator:reconciliation-error.explanation: Operator hit an error during reconciliation

operator:unavailable.explanation: Cluster operator core functionality is not working

operator:pending-csrs.explanation: Certificate Signing Requests are pending - approve them: oc get csr | grep Pending

node:not-ready.explanation: Node is in NotReady state - drilling down to find the actual cause
next_steps: Check kubelet logs on the node: oc debug node/{name} -- chroot /host journalctl -u kubelet --since '1h ago' | tail -50
Check if node is reachable: ping {name} or ssh to the node
Check dmesg for hardware errors: oc debug node/{name} -- chroot /host dmesg | tail -30
Check if disk is full: oc debug node/{name} -- chroot /host df -h
Review Red Hat KCS: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/5765631

investigation_playbook.goal: Is the node reachable at all
Is kubelet running and what does it say
commands: ssh core@{ip} 'sudo journalctl -u kubelet --since "30 min ago" --no-pager | tail -40'

investigation_playbook.goal: Check disk, memory, PID pressure
Check for hardware or kernel errors
If disk/memory full, find which component

node:disk-pressure.explanation: Node is experiencing disk pressure - drilling down to find which disk consumer is the culprit
next_steps: Check Prometheus TSDB size: oc exec -n openshift-monitoring prometheus-k8s-0 -- df -h /prometheus
Check Prometheus retention config: oc get configmap cluster-monitoring-config -n openshift-monitoring -o yaml
Check container image disk usage: oc debug node/{name} -- chroot /host crictl images | head -20
Review Red Hat KCS: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/4976801

investigation_playbook.goal: Which filesystem is full and how full
Map ALL major consumers (breadth-first, do not fixate on one)
Identify which workload owns each large pod UUID by checking volume names and cross-referencing
Identify which images or logs are consuming space
commands: ssh core@{ip} 'sudo crictl images --digests | sort -k5 -rh | head -15'

investigation_playbook.goal: Check GC and retention settings
commands: oc get configmap cluster-monitoring-config -n openshift-monitoring -o yaml 2>&1 | head -30

node:memory-pressure.explanation: Node is experiencing memory pressure - drilling down to find which process consumes memory
next_steps: Check top memory consumers on the node: oc debug node/{name} -- chroot /host ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -15
Check for OOM kills in dmesg: oc debug node/{name} -- chroot /host dmesg | grep -i oom | tail -20
Review pod resource limits in affected namespace
Review Red Hat KCS: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/5765631

investigation_playbook.goal: How much memory is used and by what type
Which processes consume the most memory
commands: oc adm top pods --field-selector spec.nodeName={name} -A --no-headers 2>&1 | sort -k3 -rh | head -15

investigation_playbook.goal: Check for OOM kills
commands: ssh core@{ip} 'sudo dmesg | grep -i oom | tail -20'

investigation_playbook.goal: Compare resource limits with actual usage for top consumers
When did memory pressure start
commands: oc get events --field-selector involvedObject.name={name} --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' 2>&1 | grep -i memory | tail -10

node:cordoned.explanation: Node has been cordoned (SchedulingDisabled) - checking why
next_steps: Check who cordoned it: oc describe node {name} | grep -A5 Taints
Check MCP status: oc get mcp - node may be updating
Check if drain was started: oc get events --field-selector involvedObject.name={name} | grep -i drain

investigation_playbook.goal: Why was the node cordoned
Is the node updating via MachineConfig
commands: oc get mcp worker -o yaml 2>&1 | grep -A10 'degradedMachineCount\|message'

investigation_playbook.goal: What triggered the cordon/drain
commands: oc get events --field-selector involvedObject.name={name} --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' 2>&1 | grep -iE 'drain|cordon|taint|schedule' | tail -10

csi:crashloop.explanation: A CSI driver controller pod is crash-looping. This can prevent volume provisioning and snapshot operations.
next_steps: Check previous container logs: oc logs <pod> -n <ns> --previous --tail=50
Check pod events: oc describe pod <pod> -n <ns>
Check if it's OOM-killed: oc get events -n <ns> --field-selector reason=OOMKilled
Check CSI driver health: oc get csidrivers
Review Red Hat KCS: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6005941

investigation_playbook.goal: Which CSI pod is crashing
commands: oc get pods -A --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -i csi | grep -v Running | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Get the crash output
commands: oc logs $(oc get pods -A --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -i csi | grep -i crash | head -1 | awk '{print "-n " $1 " " $2}') --previous --tail=50 2>&1 | tail -30

investigation_playbook.goal: Check exit code, OOM, resource limits
commands: oc describe pod $(oc get pods -A --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -i csi | grep -v Running | head -1 | awk '{print $2 " -n " $1}') 2>&1 | grep -A10 'Last State:\|Events:' | head -25

investigation_playbook.goal: Compare CSI pod resource limits with actual usage
Check storage cluster health

csi:pod-not-running.explanation: CSI-related pod is not in Running state. Check pod events and logs for details.
next_steps: Check all CSI pods: oc get pods -A | grep csi
Describe the failing pod: oc describe pod <pod> -n <ns>
Check storage cluster health: oc get storagecluster -n openshift-storage

investigation_playbook.goal: Which CSI pods are not running
commands: oc get pods -A --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -i csi | grep -v Running | head -10

investigation_playbook.goal: Pod events and status
commands: oc describe pod $(oc get pods -A --no-headers 2>&1 | grep -i csi | grep -v Running | head -1 | awk '{print $2 " -n " $1}') 2>&1 | grep -A15 Events: | head -20

investigation_playbook.goal: Overall storage cluster state

csi:driver-missing.explanation: Expected CSI driver is missing from the cluster. Storage provisioning will fail.

alert:critical.explanation: Critical alerts are firing in the cluster
